stem: A 29-year-old man with opioid use disorder (daily IV fentanyl) comes to an office-based addiction clinic to begin opioid agonist therapy. He last used 16 hours ago; his COWS score is 14 with yawning, rhinorrhea, and dilated pupils. Guidelines favour a first-line, office-based agent whose partial mu-agonism gives a ceiling on respiratory depression, and it carries an abuse-deterrent component. The clinician stresses it must NOT be dosed until objective withdrawal is present, because its high receptor affinity will displace residual full agonist from the mu receptor and precipitate withdrawal. Which agent is being described?
explanation: The described agent is a high-affinity PARTIAL mu agonist with a ceiling on respiratory depression and an abuse-deterrent naloxone component (inert sublingually, but blocks the high and precipitates withdrawal if injected) — first-line OAT per CRISM. Its high mu affinity displaces full agonists, so it precipitates withdrawal unless started once the patient is in objective withdrawal (COWS). Methadone is a FULL mu agonist with NO ceiling; it does not precipitate withdrawal on induction this way — its induction danger is accumulation/overdose and QT, managed by start-low-go-slow. Naltrexone is a mu ANTAGONIST: it would precipitate withdrawal, requires a ~7-10 day opioid-free interval, and is not agonist maintenance. Naloxone is the short-acting acute overdose RESCUE antagonist, not a maintenance OAT.
focus: partial agonist / precipitated withdrawal — start OAT induction only in objective withdrawal

stem: A 47-year-old woman has been maintained on a full mu-opioid agonist for opioid use disorder, and her dose was recently raised to 130 mg/day. She presents after a syncopal episode. ECG shows QTc 510 ms, and she was started on a macrolide antibiotic last week. The team notes the agonist she is on has no ceiling on respiratory depression, a long and variable half-life prone to accumulation, and is a CYP3A4 substrate. Which opioid agonist therapy is she receiving?
explanation: Methadone is the FULL mu agonist OAT with dose-dependent QT prolongation and torsades de pointes — an ECG (baseline and follow-up, especially above 100 mg) is standard, and a QTc-prolonging macrolide compounds the risk. It has no ceiling on respiratory depression, a long/variable half-life that causes accumulation, and is a CYP3A4 substrate (inhibitors cause toxicity, inducers precipitate withdrawal). Buprenorphine and buprenorphine/naloxone are PARTIAL agonists with a ceiling and only minimal QT effect (less than methadone), so they would not fit this dose-dependent torsades picture. Naltrexone is a mu antagonist used for maintenance, not an agonist, and does not carry this QT profile.
focus: methadone QTc / torsades caution — dose-dependent, ECG monitoring, CYP3A4 full agonist

stem: A 58-year-old man with alcohol use disorder has been abstinent for 10 days and wants pharmacotherapy to MAINTAIN abstinence. He has Child-Pugh B alcoholic cirrhosis and takes daily oral morphine for chronic pancreatitis pain; his creatinine clearance is 85 mL/min. The clinician wants an anti-craving maintenance agent that is not hepatically metabolized, is non-aversive, and will not interfere with his opioid analgesia. Which agent best fits?
explanation: Acamprosate is excreted renally UNCHANGED and is not hepatically metabolized, making it a good choice in alcoholic liver disease; it is non-aversive, best at maintaining abstinence in an already-abstinent patient, and does not block opioids — its only key limit is severe renal impairment (contraindicated at CrCl less than or equal to 30 mL/min), and his renal function is fine. Naltrexone is wrong on two counts: it is hepatotoxic AND contraindicated in patients receiving opioid analgesics (it blocks their analgesia, precipitates withdrawal, and needs an opioid-free interval). Disulfiram is AVERSIVE rather than anti-craving and is hepatotoxic (can be fulminant) — poor in cirrhosis. Naloxone is the acute overdose rescue antagonist, not an AUD maintenance drug.
focus: naltrexone vs acamprosate for AUD — renal (not hepatic) clearance, non-aversive, and the opioid-free/opioid-analgesia requirement

stem: A 52-year-old man with alcohol use disorder is motivated, insightful, and fully abstinent, and specifically asks for a medication that will make him physically ill if he drinks, as an external deterrent. He is counselled to avoid ALL alcohol — including mouthwash, cough syrup, sauces, and hand sanitizer — because accumulating acetaldehyde causes flushing, throbbing headache, vomiting, and tachycardia, and that this reaction can occur up to about two weeks after the last dose. He is warned it must never be taken while intoxicated or without his knowledge, and baseline liver enzymes are drawn. Which agent is being prescribed?
explanation: Disulfiram IRREVERSIBLY inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase, so drinking causes acetaldehyde to accumulate and produces the aversive disulfiram-alcohol reaction — it is the only AVERSIVE (not anti-craving) AUD agent, and because the enzyme block is irreversible the reaction can occur up to ~2 weeks after the last dose. It requires a motivated, abstinent patient, avoidance of all hidden alcohol sources, baseline/periodic LFTs (hepatotoxicity can be fulminant), and a boxed caution never to give it during intoxication or without the patient's knowledge. Acamprosate and naltrexone REDUCE craving rather than punishing drinking, so neither matches the aversive mechanism. Varenicline is a nicotinic partial agonist for smoking cessation and has nothing to do with alcohol aversion.
focus: disulfiram aversive mechanism (ALDH inhibition) & counselling — hidden alcohol, delayed reaction, LFTs

stem: A 44-year-old woman with a 25-pack-year smoking history and a remote history of depression wants the single most effective pharmacotherapy to stop smoking. She is started on an oral agent about a week before her chosen quit date and warned to expect nausea and vivid, abnormal dreams, and to monitor for mood or behaviour changes. The clinician explains it is a partial agonist at the alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor: partial stimulation relieves craving while blocking nicotine's reward. Which agent is this?
explanation: Varenicline is an alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor PARTIAL agonist — the most effective single pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation. It is typically started ~1 week before the quit date; nausea and vivid/abnormal dreams are its hallmark side effects, and it carries a boxed warning to monitor for neuropsychiatric symptoms (the EAGLES trial was reassuring, but monitoring is still advised, especially with a psychiatric history). Buprenorphine is a mu-OPIOID partial agonist for opioid use disorder, not a nicotinic agent. Naltrexone (opioid antagonist for alcohol/opioid use disorder) and acamprosate (anti-craving for alcohol use disorder) do not target nicotine dependence.
focus: varenicline for nicotine — alpha-4-beta-2 nicotinic partial agonist, smoking cessation
